name: APECED / APS-1
mechanismChain: AIRE loss-of-function reduces ectopic tissue-antigen expression by medullary thymic epithelial cells in the thymus.
Negative selection is weakened, so autoreactive T cells escape central tolerance.
Escaped autoreactive lymphocytes attack multiple peripheral tissues instead of remaining ignorant of self.
The result is multi-organ autoimmunity, classically with endocrine disease and chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis.

name: Omenn syndrome
mechanismChain: Hypomorphic RAG1 or RAG2 mutations impair V(D)J recombination instead of abolishing it completely.
The surviving T-cell receptor repertoire becomes severely restricted and oligoclonal, while B-cell development is poor.
Autoreactive activated T cells expand in the setting of defective immune regulation and incomplete central tolerance.
That combination produces erythroderma, enteropathy, eosinophilia, and severe immune dysregulation on a SCID spectrum.

name: IPEX
aliases: Immune dysregulation, polyendocrinopathy, enteropathy, X-linked syndrome
mechanismChain: FOXP3 loss-of-function prevents normal development and maintenance of regulatory T cells.
Without functional Tregs, activated effector T cells are not adequately suppressed in peripheral tissues.
Peripheral tolerance collapses across mucosal, endocrine, and cutaneous compartments.
The syndrome classically presents with severe enteropathy, eczema, and early endocrine autoimmunity such as type 1 diabetes.

name: IL2RA deficiency
mechanismChain: IL2RA loss removes CD25, the alpha chain of the high-affinity IL-2 receptor.
Regulatory T cells, which constitutively depend on very low concentrations of IL-2, lose a key survival and maintenance signal.
FOXP3 expression and Treg homeostasis become unstable, while activated effector cells are not properly restrained.
The resulting phenotype is IPEX-like immune dysregulation with severe enteropathy, autoimmunity, lymphoproliferation, and infection susceptibility.

name: IL2RB deficiency
mechanismChain: IL2RB defects reduce IL-2Rβ expression or signaling downstream of IL-2 and IL-15.
Regulatory T-cell support falls while NK-cell and cytotoxic lymphocyte homeostasis is also disturbed.
The immune system combines impaired control of self-reactivity with poor antiviral defense.
Infants can therefore develop multisystem autoimmunity alongside severe CMV susceptibility and immune dysregulation.

name: STAT5B deficiency
mechanismChain: STAT5B deficiency blocks a key transcriptional output of IL-2 signaling.
Regulatory T cells cannot maintain FOXP3 expression and suppressive function efficiently.
Peripheral tolerance weakens while other cytokine programs and growth hormone signaling also fail.
The phenotype mixes immune dysregulation and autoimmunity with severe postnatal growth failure.

name: CTLA-4 haploinsufficiency
mechanismChain: CTLA4 haploinsufficiency reduces the amount of CTLA-4 available on activated T cells and regulatory T cells.
CTLA-4 can no longer compete effectively with CD28 for CD80 and CD86, so costimulatory signaling stays too strong.
Activated lymphocytes persist and peripheral tolerance erodes across multiple organs.
The resulting syndrome features autoimmunity, lymphoproliferation, organ infiltration, and frequent hypogammaglobulinemia.

name: LRBA deficiency
mechanismChain: LRBA normally protects CTLA-4 from lysosomal degradation during intracellular trafficking.
When LRBA is absent, surface CTLA-4 expression falls even though the CTLA4 gene itself is intact.
That creates a functional CTLA-4 deficiency with weak checkpoint control of activated lymphocytes.
Patients develop a CTLA4-like syndrome with autoimmunity, lymphoproliferation, enteropathy, and hypogammaglobulinemia.

name: DEF6 deficiency
mechanismChain: DEF6 helps move CTLA-4-containing vesicles to the cell surface through interaction with RAB11.
DEF6 deficiency impairs CTLA-4 trafficking even when CTLA-4 protein can still be made.
Checkpoint restraint on activated T cells weakens, and germinal-center help becomes poorly controlled.
The phenotype is systemic autoimmunity with immune dysregulation that mechanistically sits close to CTLA4 and LRBA deficiency.

name: ITCH deficiency
mechanismChain: ITCH encodes an E3 ubiquitin ligase that helps impose T-cell anergy by targeting signaling proteins such as PLCγ1 and PKCθ.
It also supports regulatory T-cell generation through TIEG1-dependent control of FOXP3 expression.
Loss of ITCH therefore removes two layers of restraint: effector cells are harder to silence and Treg biology is weaker.
Patients can develop early-onset autoimmunity, chronic lung inflammation, and combined immune dysregulation.

name: BACH2-related immunodeficiency and autoimmunity
mechanismChain: BACH2 normally restrains terminal effector differentiation and limits T follicular helper cell programs.
Haploinsufficiency removes that transcriptional brake and skews lymphocytes toward pathogenic helper and plasma-cell states.
Tolerance in germinal centers becomes less stable, while immunoglobulin maturation also becomes abnormal.
The phenotype mixes autoimmunity, intestinal inflammation, and immunodeficiency.

name: Autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome
mechanismChain: FAS pathway defects impair activation-induced cell death after lymphocytes have been expanded by antigen.
Activated autoreactive lymphocytes therefore fail to undergo normal contraction and persist in circulation and lymphoid tissue.
Double-negative T cells accumulate, and self-reactive clones are not pruned efficiently.
The hallmark phenotype is chronic lymphadenopathy or splenomegaly with autoimmune cytopenias and elevated lymphoma risk.

name: CASP8 deficiency
mechanismChain: CASP8 sits downstream of death-receptor signaling and helps execute extrinsic apoptosis.
When CASP8 is absent, activated lymphocytes are harder to delete, but innate and epithelial signaling programs are also perturbed.
The disease therefore overlaps ALPS mechanistically while extending into infection susceptibility and inflammatory bowel phenotypes.
Patients can present with ALPS-like autoimmunity, lymphoproliferation, or very-early-onset inflammatory bowel disease.

name: FADD deficiency
mechanismChain: FADD is the death-domain adaptor that connects activated FAS-family receptors to the caspase machinery.
Loss of FADD blocks efficient extrinsic apoptosis, so activated lymphocytes are not culled normally.
The pathway defect also disturbs broader host-defense and inflammatory responses.
Patients show ALPS-like immune dysregulation together with recurrent invasive infection and, in some families, severe neurologic inflammation.

name: A20 haploinsufficiency
mechanismChain: TNFAIP3 encodes A20, a ubiquitin-editing enzyme that terminates NF-κB signaling downstream of inflammatory receptors.
Haploinsufficiency leaves inflammatory pathways switched on for too long after stimulation.
Innate cells overproduce cytokines such as TNF, IL-1, and IL-6, while adaptive immune tolerance is secondarily destabilized.
The phenotype often resembles early-onset Behçet disease with recurrent fever, mucosal ulcers, vasculitic features, and autoimmunity.

name: OTULIN deficiency
mechanismChain: OTULIN is the deubiquitinase that removes linear ubiquitin chains from signaling complexes.
Loss of OTULIN causes linear ubiquitin to accumulate abnormally and leaves NF-κB and TNF pathways spontaneously activated.
Myeloid cells become powerful sources of inflammatory cytokines, and proteostasis defects can spill into interferon activation.
The phenotype is early-onset systemic inflammation with skin, joint, gut, and autoimmune features.

name: RBCK1 / HOIL-1 deficiency
mechanismChain: RBCK1 encodes HOIL-1, a component of the linear ubiquitin chain assembly complex (LUBAC).
Loss of HOIL-1 destabilizes LUBAC and makes NF-κB signaling paradoxical: some cell types respond poorly while monocytes can become hyperinflammatory.
This imbalance produces both immunodeficiency and chronic inflammatory disease rather than clean immune failure in one direction.
Patients can develop autoinflammation, invasive bacterial infection, amylopectinosis, and autoimmune manifestations.

name: RNF31 / HOIP deficiency
mechanismChain: RNF31 encodes HOIP, the catalytic engine of the linear ubiquitin chain assembly complex.
HOIP deficiency impairs balanced linear ubiquitination of receptor signaling complexes and distorts NF-κB control.
As in HOIL-1 deficiency, fibroblasts can be hyporesponsive while monocytes remain overly inflammatory.
The syndrome combines immunodeficiency, autoinflammation, and dysregulated tissue inflammation.

name: IKBKG / NEMO-related immune dysregulation
mechanismChain: IKBKG encodes NEMO, the regulatory subunit of the IKK complex that enables canonical NF-κB activation.
Pathogenic variants distort how cells translate receptor stimulation into inflammatory and survival signals.
Because different cell types depend on NEMO differently, the phenotype can mix immunodeficiency with autoinflammatory or autoimmune tissue injury.
Clinical presentations span ectodermal dysplasia with immunodeficiency to NEMO-deleted exon 5 autoinflammatory syndrome with uveitis, panniculitis, and hepatitis.

name: IL-10 signaling deficiency
mechanismChain: IL-10 is a core anti-inflammatory cytokine that tells myeloid and lymphoid cells to stop amplifying mucosal inflammation.
Defects in IL10, IL10RA, or IL10RB prevent that brake from being transmitted in the intestinal immune system.
Responses to commensal bacteria remain excessive and tissue-destructive rather than resolving after stimulation.
The classic result is very-early-onset inflammatory bowel disease with fistulizing colitis and severe perianal disease.

name: Aicardi–Goutières syndrome
mechanismChain: AGS genes normally metabolize, edit, or ignore endogenous nucleic acids before they can look like viral danger signals.
When those pathways fail, self DNA or RNA accumulates in forms that activate cGAS-STING or MDA5-driven sensing.
Innate immune cells maintain a chronic type I interferon program even without infection.
The phenotype is a prototypic interferonopathy with neuroinflammation, chilblains, and lupus-like autoimmunity in some patients.

name: Familial chilblain lupus
mechanismChain: Familial chilblain lupus is a skin-dominant interferonopathy caused by abnormal handling or sensing of self nucleic acids.
Pathogenic variants in genes such as TREX1, SAMHD1, or IFIH1 keep innate antiviral pathways inappropriately active.
Type I interferon signaling becomes chronically elevated, especially in acral tissues exposed to cold or vascular stress.
The result is chilblain lesions with lupus-like autoimmunity rather than ordinary cold injury.

name: STING-associated vasculopathy with onset in infancy
mechanismChain: TMEM173 encodes STING, the adaptor that turns cytosolic DNA sensing into type I interferon production.
Gain-of-function variants activate STING constitutively even without microbial DNA.
Endothelial and immune cells are pushed into a chronic interferon-rich inflammatory state that also injures the lung.
The resulting disease combines vasculopathy, skin ulceration, and interstitial lung disease with variable autoimmunity.

name: Deficiency of ADA2
mechanismChain: ADA2 deficiency distorts extracellular adenosine biology and skews monocytes or macrophages toward a pro-inflammatory state.
Myeloid cells produce excess TNF and related inflammatory mediators, while vascular integrity and immune balance deteriorate.
The pathway defect favors vasculitis, endothelial injury, cytopenias, and immune dysregulation rather than clean adaptive autoimmunity alone.
The clinical result is a vasculopathy syndrome with strokes, livedo, cytopenias, and variable autoimmune features.

name: STAT3 gain-of-function disease
mechanismChain: Activating STAT3 mutations amplify transcriptional responses to multiple cytokines.
This reshapes T-cell differentiation and produces quantitative and qualitative defects in regulatory T cells.
Immune responses become simultaneously overactive, poorly constrained, and developmentally skewed across several organs.
Patients therefore develop early-onset multiorgan autoimmunity, lymphoproliferation, cytopenias, and inflammatory lung or gut disease.

name: STAT1 gain-of-function disease
mechanismChain: STAT1 gain-of-function mutations exaggerate transcriptional responses to interferons and related cytokines.
Persistent STAT1 signaling impairs balanced helper-cell differentiation, including IL-17 immunity, while also intensifying inflammatory set points.
Patients therefore combine infection susceptibility, especially chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis, with a substantial burden of autoimmunity.
Autoimmune thyroid disease, diabetes, hepatitis, cytopenias, lupus-like disease, and inflammatory bowel disease can all appear on this background.

name: Early classical complement deficiency
aliases: C1q / C1r / C1s / C2 / C4 deficiency with lupus-like autoimmunity
mechanismChain: The early classical complement pathway helps opsonize immune complexes and apoptotic debris for silent clearance.
When C1q, C1r, C1s, C2, or C4 are absent, self antigens linger in extracellular spaces instead of being cleared quickly.
That debris becomes a durable source of nuclear antigen for B-cell activation and autoantibody production.
The phenotype is strongly enriched for lupus or lupus-like autoimmunity, especially in C1q deficiency.

name: DNASE1L3 deficiency
mechanismChain: DNASE1L3 normally digests extracellular chromatin and membrane-associated DNA released from dying cells.
When DNASE1L3 is absent, chromatin persists in an immunogenic form instead of being rendered harmless.
B cells and other immune sensors encounter a larger, more persistent pool of nuclear self antigen.
The result is monogenic lupus with strong anti-DNA autoantibody formation and systemic inflammation.

name: DNASE1 deficiency
mechanismChain: DNASE1 helps digest extracellular DNA released into plasma and tissues.
Loss of function increases persistence of self DNA and lowers the threshold for anti-DNA immune responses.
Nuclear self antigens therefore remain available to drive autoantibody production and inflammatory immune-complex formation.
The resulting phenotype sits on the monogenic lupus spectrum.

name: PRKCD deficiency
mechanismChain: PKCδ is a signaling node that helps trigger apoptosis or deletion in autoreactive B cells.
PRKCD deficiency makes B cells resistant to B-cell receptor and calcium-dependent apoptosis.
Autoreactive clones therefore survive, proliferate, and enter antibody-producing pathways that should have been closed to them.
The phenotype is Mendelian juvenile lupus with prominent B-cell-driven autoimmunity.

name: Activated PI3K delta syndrome
mechanismChain: PIK3CD gain-of-function or PIK3R1 loss distorts the PI3K-AKT-mTOR signaling axis in lymphocytes.
Naive T-cell and B-cell pools shrink, transitional B cells expand, and lymphocytes become developmentally skewed and prematurely exhausted.
The immune system therefore combines poor host defense with persistent lymphoid activation and unstable tolerance.
Clinically this produces recurrent sinopulmonary infection, lymphoproliferation, enteropathy, cytopenias, and antibody dysregulation.
